title: DNS Database
summary: A large Go backend shaped around lifecycle design, documentation, and the belief that software should remain understandable years later.
purpose: Long-term engineering craftsmanship rather than novelty: explicit lifecycles, durable storage, documented decisions, and tests that make change safer.
overview: DNS Database is the clearest engineering record of sustained work in this archive: a Go backend of approximately 13,000 lines, backed by RocksDB and supported by extensive documentation and roughly 90 percent test coverage.

Its defining concern is lifecycle design. Storage, behavior, tests, and documentation are treated as parts of one system that must remain understandable after the original implementation context has faded.

architecture.label: Application core
architecture.detail: A substantial Go backend organized around explicit, lifecycle-focused behavior.

architecture.label: Persistence
architecture.detail: RocksDB provides the storage foundation and makes data lifecycle decisions a first-class architectural concern.

architecture.label: Confidence
architecture.detail: Approximately 90% test coverage supports change, while extensive documentation preserves intent beyond the code.

concerns: Safe change over a long project life
represents: Long-term engineering craftsmanship: not only making a backend work, but making its behavior and history possible to understand years later.

title: Test Generation Platform
summary: Transforms human-written test cases into executable Playwright automation.
purpose: Bridge the language people use to describe quality with the automation systems used to verify it, without making either side incidental.
overview: The Test Generation Platform addresses the distance between a test case written for people and automation written for execution. Human-readable cases originate in KiwiTCMS and are transformed toward executable Playwright tests.
The work combines Python, TypeScript, Docker, Jinja2, KiwiTCMS, and Playwright. The central challenge is preserving test intent while moving between human and executable representations.

architecture.label: Human source
architecture.detail: KiwiTCMS keeps test intent in a form that remains readable and maintainable by people.

architecture.label: Transformation
architecture.detail: Python and Jinja2 participate in producing understandable automation rather than opaque generated output.

architecture.label: Execution
architecture.detail: Playwright and TypeScript provide the browser-automation target; Docker supports repeatable environments.

architecture.label: Extension boundary
architecture.detail: The External Playwright Adapter lets independent repositories participate without erasing their ownership boundaries.

represents: A bridge between how people describe quality and how systems execute it.

title: External Playwright Adapter
summary: Allows external repositories to participate in a larger testing ecosystem.
purpose: A pragmatic boundary: teams can keep tests near their own code while still taking part in shared execution and reporting workflows.
facts: Part of a broader testing ecosystem
overview: The External Playwright Adapter is the pragmatic edge of the larger test-generation ecosystem. It exists so repositories outside the central system can participate in shared testing workflows.
Its significance is architectural rather than visual: integration should respect the boundary of the external repository instead of forcing every test suite into one ownership model.

architecture.label: External ownership
architecture.detail: Tests can remain close to the repositories and teams responsible for them.

architecture.label: Shared participation
architecture.detail: Independent Playwright suites can take part in the broader execution and reporting ecosystem.

architecture.label: Repeatability
architecture.detail: TypeScript, Playwright, and Docker form the known technical context for the adapter.

represents: Pragmatic integration: a small boundary component that makes a larger system usable in the real world.

title: JARVIS
summary: A personal system for reducing context switching across projects, memory, documentation, automation, and creative work.
purpose: Bring the different parts of long-running work into one understandable context while preserving human control at meaningful decision points.
facts: Keep consequential decisions behind explicit gates
technologyNote: Planned architecture; expected to evolve with the project.
overview: JARVIS is a personal orchestration platform for reducing context switching across software projects, memory, documentation, automation, and creative work.

The project is where several recurring concerns converge: preserving context, connecting records, letting automation carry work forward, and keeping human responsibility visible at consequential decisions.

architecture.label: Orchestration
architecture.detail: LangGraph and LiteLLM are part of the planned foundation for coordinated workflows and model access.

architecture.label: Service and interface
architecture.detail: FastAPI and React are planned as the service and interaction layers.

architecture.label: Memory and durable data
architecture.detail: ChromaDB and PostgreSQL are planned for retrieval-oriented memory and structured persistence.

architecture.label: Control principle
architecture.detail: Autonomous Until The Gate allows routine work to advance while explicit human gates protect consequential choices.

concerns: Keeping creative and engineering work in one understandable context
represents: The convergence of systems thinking across domains, with autonomy treated as a responsibility and architecture problem rather than a novelty.
